symbols: sword — instrument of divine justice and Judith's role as God's agent of deliverance
red drapery — bloodshed and the violent act of decapitation, also royal authority
ornate jewelry and headdress — Judith's deliberate use of beauty as a weapon to seduce and overcome Holofernes
white garment partially open — vulnerability weaponized through seduction, purity beneath the act of war
confident downward gaze — resolute composure of a woman who has fulfilled God's mission
description: A half-length portrait of a dark-haired woman standing before rich crimson drapery. She wears a white garment loosely draped over her shoulders, an ornate jeweled headdress, necklace with colored stones, bracelets, and rings. A wide embroidered belt cinches her waist. Her left hand rests on her hip while her right hand grips the hilt of a short sword or dagger at her side. Her expression is calm and resolute, gazing slightly downward with composed authority.
theological_description: Judith is one of the great deliverer figures of the deuterocanonical tradition. The Book of Judith recounts how she entered the Assyrian camp, beguiled the general Holofernes with her beauty, and beheaded him in his tent, saving the besieged city of Bethulia. The painting captures the moment after the deed: Judith stands with sword in hand, her composure reflecting the confidence of one who acted as God's instrument. Patristic and medieval exegesis read Judith as a type of the Church militant triumphing over evil, and as a Marian figure — the humble woman who crushes the head of the serpent (Genesis 3:15). Her use of beauty and cunning echoes Jael's slaying of Sisera (Judges 4:21), forming a pattern of God using unexpected agents — women — to defeat mighty oppressors. The sword symbolizes divine justice executed through human courage, and the red drapery evokes the bloodshed necessary for Israel's salvation.

scripture_refs.reason: Judith takes Holofernes' sword and strikes his neck twice, cutting off his head — the central narrative depicted
Judith adorns herself with jewelry and fine garments to captivate Holofernes — reflected in her ornate appearance
Jael's slaying of Sisera prefigures Judith's act — both women deliver Israel by killing an enemy commander
The woman who crushes the head of the enemy — Judith as type of the protoevangelium promise
Judith's victory hymn: God delivered the enemy not by warriors but by the hand of a woman
